required.title: [Materials] Real bricks (at least 20-30), assorted stones, concrete blocks (half-blocks for smaller hands)
[Tools] A torpedo level, a tape measure, a pencil, a trowel (small mason's trowel for younger hands), a bucket, a string line

suggested.title: [Visit] A local stone wall, brick building, or historical masonry structure

competence_markers.description: The student can explain why staggering joints (running bond) is stronger than stacking joints in alignment
The student can use a level to achieve level (horizontal) and plumb (vertical) conditions and explain why both matter
The student understands that mortar bonds bricks together and that wall strength depends on both the bricks and the mortar
The student has built a dry-stack wall or border of at least 3 courses using running bond, with the first course level
The student has built or laid a stone or brick pathway that is stable and walkable
The student demonstrates patience with masonry work — accepting the slow pace, checking level frequently, adjusting placement rather than rushing

content.why_this_matters:
Pick up a brick. Feel how heavy it is. Feel how hard it is. Now think about this: almost every old building you have ever seen — every castle, every cathedral, every ancient wall still standing after hundreds or thousands of years — was built from pieces like the one in your hand. One brick at a time. One stone at a time. Stacked by people who understood how to make heavy things stay where they put them.

Masonry is the art of building with individual units — bricks, stones, concrete blocks — arranged and joined so they become something much stronger than any single piece. A single brick is just a brick. A thousand bricks laid correctly are a wall that can stand for centuries. The difference is not the bricks. The difference is how they are put together.

In this packet, you will handle real bricks and stones. You will stack them and discover the rules of stability — why some arrangements stand and others fall. You will learn about mortar, the material that holds bricks together. You will use a level and discover that "level" and "plumb" are not approximate — they are exact conditions that the entire structure depends on. And you will build something with your own hands from materials that humans have built with for ten thousand years.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Material awareness** — understanding that bricks and stones are heavy, hard, permanent building units with specific properties
2. **Stability principles** — discovering intuitively why wide bases are more stable, why staggering joints is stronger, and why height without width falls
3. **Leveling and plumb** — using a level to achieve true horizontal and true vertical
4. **Mortar concepts** — understanding what mortar is, what it does, and why the strength of a wall depends on both the bricks and the mortar
5. **Patience** — masonry teaches patience like no other trade; you cannot rush curing, you cannot undo mortared joints easily, and you must get the foundation right because everything above depends on it
content.core_content:
### Part 1: What Bricks and Stones Are

A brick is a block of clay that has been shaped and fired (baked at very high temperature) until it becomes hard and permanent. Bricks have been made for over 5,000 years. The bricks in Roman walls are still solid. The bricks in your neighbor's house will outlast the wood framing around them.

A stone is a natural piece of rock. Fieldstones are gathered from the ground. Quarried stone is cut from bedrock. Stone has been used for building since before recorded history — Gobekli Tepe, the oldest known structure, is made of stone and is roughly 12,000 years old.

A concrete block (CMU — concrete masonry unit) is a modern manufacturing product: a mixture of cement, aggregate, and water, molded and cured into standard-sized blocks. Concrete blocks are lighter than solid bricks (because they are hollow) but still very strong in compression.

What all these materials share: they are heavy, hard, and strong under compression (squeezing force). They last a very long time when used correctly. And they are not easily changed once placed — which means you must place them correctly the first time.

### Part 2: Stability — Why Some Stacks Stand and Others Fall

Build a tower of bricks. Stack them directly on top of each other, edges aligned. Notice that the tower wobbles and eventually falls — especially when it gets tall.

Now build a wall. Lay the first row (course) flat. For the second course, offset the bricks so that each brick in the second row spans the gap between two bricks in the first row. This is called a running bond — the most basic brick pattern. Notice that this wall is much more stable than the straight tower.

**Why?** When you stack bricks directly on top of each other, every vertical joint lines up. A crack or a force can travel straight down through the aligned joints and the wall splits. When you stagger the joints (running bond), every vertical joint in one course is covered by solid brick in the next course. Force cannot travel in a straight line through the wall — it is distributed across multiple bricks. The wall acts as a unit, not as a stack of individual pieces.

**Other stability principles you will discover:**
- A wide base is more stable than a narrow one (this is why pyramids are the most stable of all structures — they are wider at the bottom than at the top)
- Height without width tips over (a tall, narrow wall falls in wind; a short, wide wall does not)
- Weight at the bottom stabilizes the structure (heavy base courses resist tipping)

### Part 3: Level and Plumb

**Level** means perfectly horizontal — parallel to the surface of still water. **Plumb** means perfectly vertical — the direction gravity pulls (the direction a string hangs when you hold one end).

In masonry, level and plumb are not approximate. If your first course is not level, every course above it will be off — and the error compounds. A wall that is 1/4 inch out of level at the bottom can be 2 inches out of plumb at the top. That wall may fall.

**Using a level:**
1. Place the level on the surface you are checking
2. Look at the bubble in the vial
3. If the bubble is centered between the two lines, the surface is level (or plumb, if you are checking vertical)
4. If the bubble is off-center, the surface is not level — the high side is the side the bubble moves toward

Practice with your level on surfaces around your home. Is the kitchen counter level? Is the door frame plumb? You will be surprised how many surfaces in everyday life are not quite true — and you will begin to notice it.

### Part 4: Mortar — The Material Between the Bricks

Mortar is a paste made from cement, sand, and water. When it is fresh, it is soft and workable — you can spread it, shape it, and press bricks into it. Over hours and days, it hardens into a solid material that bonds the bricks together.

Mortar is not glue. It works by filling the gaps between the rough surfaces of the bricks, creating a mechanical bond (the mortar fills the tiny holes and irregularities in the brick surface) and a chemical bond (the cement in the mortar adheres to the brick surface as it cures).

The strength of a masonry wall depends on both the bricks and the mortar. Strong bricks with weak mortar produce a weak wall. Good bricks with no mortar produce a dry-stack wall that relies only on gravity and friction — it works for garden walls and fences but not for structures that must resist wind or carry roof loads.

At Stretching Branches, you will not mix or use mortar. You will build dry-stack structures — bricks and stones placed without mortar. This teaches the principles of stability, bond pattern, and level without the complexity of mortar work. Mortar comes at Weathering Storms.

### Part 5: Building with What the Earth Gives You

Every civilization that built with stone started by looking at what the earth provided. In Egypt, limestone was quarried from the banks of the Nile. In England, flint and chalk were available in the south, granite in the north. In New England, fieldstone walls were built from the rocks that glaciers deposited and that farmers pulled from their fields every spring.

When you gather stones for your dry-stack project, you are doing exactly what builders have done for thousands of years: looking at the available material, assessing its shape and size, and figuring out how to make it into something useful. Flat stones stack well. Round stones are unstable. Large stones go at the bottom. Small stones fill gaps. This is not engineering theory — it is common sense refined by experience. And it is the same common sense that built the walls of Machu Picchu and the foundations of medieval cathedrals.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Stability Understanding
**Developing well:** The student uses running bond without being told after discovering that aligned stacking is unstable. They build with wider bases. They can explain in their own words why staggered joints are stronger.

**Developing slowly:** The student continues stacking in alignment despite repeated failures. They cannot articulate why one arrangement is more stable than another.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student had enough free-building time to discover the principles? If you explained the rules before they discovered them, you may have short-circuited the learning. Give them bricks and time.

### 2. Level and Plumb
**Developing well:** The student uses the level frequently and adjusts placement until the bubble is centered. They check level on each course before adding the next.

**Developing slowly:** The student places the level once, notes it is not level, and continues building anyway. Or they cannot read the bubble consistently.

### 3. Patience
**Developing well:** The student works methodically — checking each piece, adjusting, checking again. They accept that masonry is slow work and do not rush.

**Developing slowly:** The student rushes, skips level checks, and becomes frustrated when the wall is not straight. Patience with masonry develops over time — multiple sessions are often needed.
